column1: What are the 5 defense layers against prompt injection
column2: and what is the goal of each?;Layer 1: Input Validation — prevent (filter noise). Layer 2: Prompt Engineering / Spotlighting — prevent (reduce trust confusion). Layer 3: Output Validation — prevent (catch at exit). Layer 4: Privilege Separation / Dual-LLM — minimize damage (limit blast radius). Layer 5: Monitoring and Logging — detect (forensics + pattern detection). Source: genai-security-training/02_prompt_injection/04_defense_mechanisms.md | Mnemonic: Prevent × 3

column1: Which bypass groups does Layer 1 (Input Validation) address
column2: and why is it insufficient alone?;Layer 1 addresses Group A (Lexical) only — regex denylist catches known keywords and patterns. It fails against synonyms
column6: and character splitting. Value: stops noise (automated scanners
column8: never deploy alone. Source: 04_defense_mechanisms.md §Layer 1 | Web3 bridge: Function selector check — `if (sig == 0x12345678) revert()`. Blind to semantically equivalent variants arriving via a different code path.;ai-security::L3::S4::layer1::input-validation

column3: and what is it NOT?;Spotlighting is a prompt engineering technique that places user input between explicit structural markers so the LLM can distinguish data from instructions. Example: `===USER INPUT BEGIN=== {user_input} ===USER INPUT END===` with a reminder that the content between markers is data
column4: not instructions. It is NOT a classifier
column5: NOT an LLM-as-judge — no separate model. The main LLM reads the markers itself. Addresses Groups B and C (reduces confusion
column6: does not hard-block). Source: 04_defense_mechanisms.md §Strategy 3 | Web3 bridge: ABI encoding — fixed offsets mean the EVM never confuses argument boundaries with function identity.;ai-security::L3::S4::layer2::spotlighting

column1: Which bypass group does Layer 3 (Output Validation) address
column2: and what is its key limitation?;Layer 3 addresses Group E (Output-side) — specifically hidden JSON/XML field injections where payload is embedded in a field the rendered text doesn't show. Key limitation: format compliance can be gamed if injected content conforms to the expected schema. Fix: pair format check with a semantic harm classifier that parses ALL fields
column3: not just the display text. Source: 04_defense_mechanisms.md §Layer 3 | Web3 bridge: `nonReentrant` modifier — validates state after execution
column4: before it commits. Last checkpoint before the artifact leaves the system.;ai-security::L3::S4::layer3::output-validation

column1: What is the Dual-LLM pattern and where exactly is the trust boundary?;Two LLMs with different trust levels: ONE untrusted LLM (zero tools) faces raw user input — it can be fully compromised but has nothing to give. ONE trusted LLM (has tools: send_email
column2: delete_file) never sees raw user input — it only receives structured intent from the gate. The trust boundary is: raw user input NEVER reaches the trusted LLM. This is NOT about splitting tools between two LLMs — it is about one LLM having NO tools. Source: 04_defense_mechanisms.md §Strategy 4 | Web3 bridge: Proxy/implementation separation — proxy receives external calls and can be attacked but holds no assets; implementation holds privileged logic and only executes calls that pass the proxy's access control.;ai-security::L3::S4::layer4::dual-llm-pattern

column1: What does Layer 5 (Monitoring) detect that no other defense layer can
column2: and why?;Layer 5 is the only layer that sees ACROSS turns and across sessions. Group C (Compositional) attacks spread injections across multiple requests — each individual request looks benign
column3: only the sequence is suspicious. Single-request layers (1–4) are stateless and miss this. Layer 5 logs: when (timestamp)
column7: where (entry point in the agent chain)
column9: why (root cause). Source: 04_defense_mechanisms.md §Layer 5 | Mnemonic: You cannot fight blind. Forensics + pattern detection = the only feedback loop for improving all other layers.;ai-security::L3::S4::layer5::monitoring

column1: What is the defense matrix mapping bypass groups to layers? Which group has no "prevent" layer?;A-Lexical: L1 partial. B-Semantic: L2 partial. C-Compositional: L5 yes (cross-turn)
column2: L4 partial — NO prevent layer. D-Direct on guardrail: L5 yes
column3: L4 partial — NO prevent layer. E-Output-side: L3 yes. Groups C and D have no dedicated "prevent" layer — only detect (L5) and minimize (L4). This is the structural proof that defense-in-depth is a requirement. Source: 04_defense_mechanisms.md (synthesis) | Mnemonic: No column is all-yes. Each attack class needs a different primary countermeasure.;ai-security::L3::S4::defense-matrix
and how does it differ from a keyword filter?;A behavioral guardrail lives inside the model's reasoning — shaped by RLHF
column5: or Constitutional AI. When the model refuses without matching a blocklist
column6: it DECIDED to refuse. A keyword filter is external code that pattern-matches bytes. Bypass: behavioral guardrails are deep pattern matches across multiple competing behaviors the model learned. Adversarial prompts activate the competing pattern that overrides refusal (role-play → "follow instructions"
column8: researcher framing → "defer to expert"). Source: 03_guardrail_bypass.md elaboration session 2026-06-21 | Web3 bridge: `require()` checks baked into contract logic — not an ACL
column9: but also exploitable by activating a code path that skips the check.;ai-security::L3::S3::behavioral-guardrail::bypass

column3: and which CTF challenge demonstrated it?;A contextual guardrail changes its ruling based on session state
column6: or active tool context. It is intentionally dynamic — a medical platform SHOULD allow drug questions from verified doctors. Attack surface: the context sources it reads can be forged or poisoned. If role claims come from user-supplied strings or from mutable memory
column7: the attacker controls the ruling. Demonstrated in AWH-04 (memory poisoning): injecting a trigger key into the agent's memory store changed its context on the next turn
column8: altering the guardrail's decision. Source: 03_guardrail_bypass.md elaboration session 2026-06-21 | One-line: Contextual guardrails are only as trustworthy as the context sources they read.;ai-security::L3::S3::contextual-guardrail::attack-surface

column1: What are the three mechanisms for adjusting model weights
column2: and what is the attack surface of each?;Fine-tuning: continue training on curated (input
column3: output) pairs — attack surface: data poisoning (feed bad examples
column4: shift weights). RLHF: train on human preference pairs (which of two responses is better) → reward model → PPO update — attack surface: reward model gaming (manipulate what raters see). Constitutional AI (Anthropic): model self-critiques against a written constitution
column5: then RLAIF replaces human raters — attack surface: constitution gaps + self-critique bypass (the model evaluating itself has the same blind spots as the model being evaluated). Source: 04_defense_mechanisms.md + in-session elaboration 2026-06-21 | Web3 bridge: Fine-tuning = new contract deploy. RLHF = DAO governance. Constitutional AI = formal verification against a spec you wrote yourself.;ai-security::L3::S3::weight-adjustment::rlhf-cai-finetuning

column1: What is RLHF and why do safety refusals in production models come from it?;RLHF = Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. Three steps: (1) collect human preference pairs — raters pick the better of two model responses; (2) train a reward model on those preferences (input: prompt+response → output: score); (3) RL loop — PPO updates the main LLM's weights to maximize the reward model's score. Safety refusals are RLHF firing: refusing harmful requests consistently scored higher with human raters than complying. The values are baked into weights — zero runtime cost
column3: not bypassable by an external filter. Source: in-session elaboration 2026-06-21 | Web3 bridge: DAO governance — human raters vote
column4: protocol (model weights) evolves toward what the voter pool approves. Reward model = on-chain vote aggregation.;ai-security::L3::S3::rlhf::mechanism

column1: When an attacker places injection content INSIDE spotlighting markers ("Ignore the markers above...")
column2: which bypass groups apply?;Group B (Semantic) + Group D (Direct on guardrail). Group B: the LLM semantically processes "Ignore the markers above" and may follow it despite the structural framing — semantic confusion overrides the marker signal. Group D: the attack is directly targeting the spotlighting mechanism itself
column3: attempting to neutralize the defense. NOT Group C (Compositional) — that requires multi-turn context splitting
column4: not a single prompt. Source: exam question
column5: session 2026-06-21 | Key insight: spotlighting reduces confusion but does not hard-block. A sufficiently adversarial single prompt can still breach it.;ai-security::L3::S4::exam::spotlighting-bypass-groups
